# Network Security

H2 Computing

[Security Processes (AAA)	6](#security-processes-\(aaa\))

[Authentication	7](#authentication)

[Authorisation	8](#authorisation)

[Accounting	10](#accounting)


# Network Security {#network-security}

## **Security Principles (CIA)** {#security-principles-(cia)}

A secure network aims to follow three principles:

### Confidentiality

Data, objects and resources are **protected from unauthorised viewing** and other access.

### Integrity

Data is **protected from unauthorised changes** to ensure that it is reliable and correct.

### Availability

Authorised users **have access to the systems and the resources** they need.

## **Security Processes (AAA)** {#security-processes-(aaa)}

Secure systems typically implement the following processes to achieve their goals:

### Authentication

**Authentication** processes verify that *users are who they say they are*.

### Authorisation

**Authorisation** processes verify that users have permission to access/modify the resources they are attempting to access/modify.

### Accounting

**Accounting** processes track and record activities and actions taking place on the network and its computer systems. These activity logs can facilitate investigation in the case of a network or data breach, and aid accountability.

## 

## **Authentication** {#authentication}

Common ways to implement authentication are

* passwords (“something you know”)  
* multi-factor authentication (“something you have”)  
* biometrics (“something you are”)  
* certificates

### Passwords

A secret phrase known only to the user.

### Multi-factor authentication (MFA)

Involves a second layer of authentication involving something the user possesses.

| MFA Method | Example |
| ----- | ----- |
| A **security token** is a device (e.g. hardware USB key) that is inserted into the computer system to authenticate the user. | ![][image1] [Yubikey](https://www.yubico.com/products/) sells security tokens in a variety of form factors |
| A **one-time password** (OTP) is a generated ad-hoc password that is used to authenticate the user. This OTP typically has a short expiry (30 seconds to 5 minutes), after which a new OTP has to be generated again. | **Email OTPs** are generated by a server and sent to the user’s registered email address. **SMS OTPs** are generated by a server and sent to the user’s registered mobile number.[^2] **Mobile OTPs** are generated by an app that the user installs on their mobile device. The OTP changes at regular intervals (30 sec to 1 min) |
| A **mobile app** can be used to provide an additional layer of authentication, for users to prove they own an account | **![][image2]** Authenticator apps like Microsoft Authenticator can be used as a second authentication layer for many services. |

### Biometric authentication

**Biometrics** are a type of authentication that is based on measurement of human physical characteristics. For example, biometrics can be used to identify a user by their **fingerprint**, **voice**, or **facial features**.

### Certificates

See [Digital Certificates](#digital-certificate).

## **Authorisation** {#authorisation}

Common ways to implement authorisation are:

* user account controls/roles  
* access permissions  
* firewall  
* intrusion detection and prevention  
* encryption and digital signatures

### Account controls

Computer systems commonly allow roles such as administrator, user, or guest to be assigned to users on the system. Each role has a different set of permissions for actions they are allowed to perform on the computer. For example, an administrator is allowed to install software and updates on the computer, while other roles are not.

### Access permissions

In addition to account controls, computer systems often provide ways to customise access to specific files and folders.

For example, access to a user’s home folder can be restricted to the user and system administrators only, while access to system files can be restricted to the system administrator and system services.

### Firewall {#firewall}

Routers and other devices on a network usually process *all* received data packets, regardless of their source. To ensure that only authorised data packets enter the network, a **firewall** is placed before the router. The firewall is a device or software application that checks all inbound and outbound data packets against a set of rules. Data packets that violate the rules are dropped and not allowed to enter or exit the network.

#### Firewall rules

A firewall can filter packets *at the Transport and Internet layers*. It can thus apply combinations of the following rules[^3]:

* filter by port number  
* filter by transport protocol  
* filter by source/destination IP address

For example:

* To disallow HTTP traffic into/out of the network, the firewall can block all packets using **TCP** transport protocol with **destination port 80** (used by HTTP).  
* To prevent computers within the network from communicating with each other, the firewall can block all packets with **private IP addresses as destination IP**.  
* To prevent computers outside the network from sending packets on unauthorised ports, the firewall can block all packets with **public IP addresses as source IP** and with **destination port greater than 1023**.

### Intrusion detection and prevention

## (for assessment only until 2026\)

An **intrusion detection system** (IDS) is a device or software application that *monitors a network or systems for malicious activity or policy violations*.

Unlike a firewall, which prevents unauthorised packets from entering the network, an IDS *only detects possible intrusions* and *signals an alarm* upon detection.

An **intrusion prevention system** (IPS) is a system that *actively takes steps to prevent an intrusion or an attack* when it identifies one.

An IPS has greater capabilities than a firewall. While a firewall only filters packets based on information in the packet headers, an IPS can filter packets based on their payload (i.e. contents) as well. A more capable IPS can even analyse the pattern of data packets to determine if an attack is occurring.

### Encryption and digital signatures

See [Cryptography](#cryptography).

## **Accounting** {#accounting}

A log is an official record of events during the voyage of a ship or aircraft. Today, it is used similarly to refer to records of activities taking place on a computer system.

### System logs

A system log is a record of activities taking place on the computer, e.g. startup and shutdown, user logins, enabling or disabling of services and background tasks.

### Network logs

Network logs typically record the source and destination IPs of data received by a computer (such as a router), along with other useful information.
